name: Mesh
summary: Bobby Powers et al.'s allocator that implements compaction without relocation — finds pairs of sparsely-occupied pages with disjoint live-object bitmaps and merges them at the *physical* page level via `mremap`, leaving virtual addresses unchanged.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Bobby Powers et al.'s Mesh (PLDI 2019) implements "compaction without relocation" — the unique idea that a non-moving allocator can still defragment by merging the *physical* pages backing two virtual pages whose live-object bitmaps happen to be disjoint. Pointer values do not change; the kernel just maps two virtual pages to the same physical page after the merge.

Mesh uses only standard virtual-memory operations (no kernel changes) so it works as a drop-in `LD_PRELOAD` library. It is the existence proof that virtual memory can substitute for a moving collector in C/C++ programs that cannot tolerate pointer changes.

sections.title: Bitmap-disjoint page merging via mremap
sections.content:
Mesh tracks per-page allocation bitmaps. Periodically it searches for pairs of sparsely-occupied pages whose live-object bitmaps are disjoint and merges them: `mremap` collapses the two virtual pages onto a single physical page, the unused physical page is returned to the OS, and live objects in both pages remain at their original virtual addresses. Randomized allocation plus a randomized pair-search algorithm provably breaks the Robson worst-case fragmentation bound with high probability.

The implementation is built on top of Heap Layers. Reported numbers: Firefox memory reduced by 16%, Redis by 39%, with comparable runtime to state-of-the-art allocators.

sections.title: Status
sections.content: Status (as of 2026-04): research-grade implementation available on GitHub; not in widespread production deployment, but the technique itself is the headline contribution. The "compact without moving" idea is unique in the allocator literature — virtual memory is what makes it possible, and the algorithmic proof connecting random search to fragmentation bounds is a standalone result.
